content:
Ryan, here’s what I’d do—quietly, simply, and with a clear conscience.

During the reading of Psalm 19
- Put an arm around my 10-year-old and whisper something like: “Listen to this—God’s world is talking. The sky is telling the truth about Him. God really made all of this.” 
- After verses 7–11 are read, add: “The same God who speaks through the heavens speaks perfectly in His Word. We trust both, and we let the Bible tell us what the world means.”

After the service, with my child
- Open Psalm 19 and Genesis 1 side by side. Explain:
  - Poetry uses pictures to say what is true (the sun isn’t literally a bridegroom, but God’s glory is truly seen in it).
  - Genesis 1 is not Hebrew poetry like the Psalms; it’s highly structured, exalted prose. Its artistry doesn’t make it less true—it makes it more memorable. God really created by His word, He ordered the days, He made humans in His image, and He called it good.
- Show a few anchors so they see how the Bible speaks with one voice:
  - Genesis 1:1; Psalm 33:6,9; John 1:1–3; Hebrews 11:3; Exodus 20:11.
- Teach this principle: We don’t change how we read the Bible to avoid awkwardness. We read it the way it asks to be read, and we love people who differ. Unity comes from truth spoken in love (Ephesians 4:3, 15).

How I speak about the elder, in front of my child
- I won’t roll my eyes or sow suspicion. I’ll say, “Our elder wants peace in the church. We want that too. Christians do disagree on some details about ‘how long’ and ‘how’—and we can show kindness there—but we never bend what Scripture plainly teaches. Let’s pray for our leaders and be like the Bereans who tested everything by the Scriptures (Acts 17:11).”

Sometime that week, with the elder
- Ask for a gracious, private conversation. “When you said, ‘Read Genesis 1 as poetic for the sake of unity,’ did you mean ‘beautiful, exalted language that is still reporting what God actually did,’ or did you mean we should treat it as non-historical? I long for unity too, but not at the expense of what the text itself claims.” 
- Appeal to the pattern of Scripture interpreting Scripture (e.g., Exodus 20:11 treats the creation days as the ground of our week) and to the call to teach the whole counsel of God (Acts 20:27). Suggest a pastoral path that protects consciences without flattening the text.

Bottom line I want my child to hear as Psalm 19 is read
- God really made the world.
- The world really tells the truth about Him.
- God’s Word tells the truth perfectly.
- We submit to Scripture, speak the truth in love, and trust Christ—the true “stumbling block” God Himself has set (1 Corinthians 1:23).
